pick.rationale: Vanderbilt holds a commanding ~15.6-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage over McNeese (KenPom #12, AdjEM +28.12 vs. KenPom #67, AdjEM +12.52), with the 6th-best offense in the country (AdjOE 127.3) built against elite SEC competition that McNeese's Southland schedule cannot replicate. The Commodores arrive in peak form after stunning #4 Florida 91-74 in the SEC Tournament semifinals, demonstrating elite offensive firepower and tournament-ready execution, and the 11.5-point betting spread (-650 ML) reflects broad market consensus on their superiority. McNeese is a genuinely dangerous 12-seed — 28-5 with the nation's #1 turnover defense and a 41.2% model-assigned upset probability from The Athletic — and the historically high 5/12 upset rate (~35%) warrants real respect, but the efficiency gap, Vanderbilt's SEC battle-testing, and their late-season momentum make the Commodores the right side in this matchup.

reasoningStep.title: Vanderbilt vs. McNeese — 5/12 South Region Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Vanderbilt is a clear but not overwhelming favorite based on a ~15.6-point efficiency gap, elite offense ranked 6th nationally, SEC battle-testing, and strong late-season momentum including a win over Florida in the SEC Tournament. McNeese is a legitimate upset threat with the nation's #1 turnover defense, a 28-5 record, and a 41.2% model-assigned upset probability, but the Southland schedule and coaching change limit their ceiling against a top-12 national program.
evidence: Vanderbilt: KenPom #12, AdjEM +28.12, AdjOE 127.3 (6th nationally), AdjDE 99.2 (28th), 26-7 SEC record. McNeese: KenPom #67, AdjEM +12.52, AdjOE 114.3 (91st), AdjDE 101.8, 28-5 Southland record — approximately 15.6-point efficiency gap.
Vanderbilt is an 11.5-point favorite at -650 ML; McNeese is +475, per BetMGM. Game scheduled March 19, 2026 on truTV.
Vanderbilt stunned #4 Florida 91-74 in the SEC Tournament semifinals on March 14, 2026 — a major statement win demonstrating elite offensive firepower and tournament readiness.

The Athletic's model assigns McNeese a 41.2% upset probability, identifying them as the second-favorite Bracket Breaker — driven by the nation's #1 turnover defense and strong KenPom profile for a 12-seed.
McNeese was the #1 team in KenPom's Southland metrics all season with elite two-way turnover profile, but head coach departed before the tournament with two key starters returning.

Log In Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Become a member Become a member Home Articles & Editorials Ticket Punched: Previewing the McNeese Cowboys For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles & Editorials Articles & Editorials Bracketology Bracketology Free Content Free Content Ticket Punched: Previewing the McNeese Cowboys For the 2026 NCAA Tournament Featured Mar 13, 2026 by Matthew Winick The head coach may be gone, but two key starters remain, and McNeese is headed back to the NCAA Tournament after blowing the doors off top-seed Stephen F. Austin in the Southland finals. Though just the second seed in the league, McNeese had been the No. 1 team in KenPom's metrics for the entirety of the season, off the backs of the nation's number one turnover defense and one of the best two-way turnover margins in the country. The Cowboys had just two losses in non-conference play, both to surefire NCAA Tournament teams in Michigan and Santa Clara. They've also really found their footing of late, winning 10 straight games. Perhaps the craziest part about McNeese's success off of last year's Round of 32 run with now-NC State head coach Will Wade is that their two returning starters, Javohn Garcia and DJ Richards, aren't either of their two top scorers. Instead, that honor belongs to transfer guards Larry Johnson (Creighton), and Tyshawn Archie (Tulsa). Almost an embarrassment of riches, nine of McNeese's 10 rotation players have played at either the power conference or upper mid-major level, including former heralded recruits Garwey Dual and Jerrell Colbert. That said, it's not all domination for the Cowboys. They needed three overtimes to take down UT Rio Grande Valley in the semifinals, and often have serious issues scoring the ball when they can't get out in transition off their steals. Still, if last year is any indication, McNeese is not a team that a 5-seed wants to see next to their name on Selection Sunday. Distinct Defensive Identity KenPom provides 18 different defensive metrics in the scouting report section of his site. Hilariously, McNeese is either top-100, or sub-200 in every single one. No mediocrity for this group! This is all due to the fact that no team pressures the ball more than McNeese. The team is first in defensive turnover rate as previously mentioned, but also first in steal rate, sixth in block rate, and first in longest defensive possessions allowed at 19.2 seconds. Perhaps the craziest part about McNeese's success off of last year's Round of 32 run with now-NC State head coach Will Wade is that their two returning starters, Javohn Garcia and DJ Richards, aren't either of their two top scorers. Instead, that honor belongs to transfer guards Larry Johnson (Creighton), and Tyshawn Archie (Tulsa). Almost an embarrassment of riches, nine of McNeese's 10 rotation players have played at either the power conference or upper mid-major level, including former heralded recruits Garwey Dual and Jerrell Colbert. They needed three overtimes to take down UT Rio Grande Valley in the semifinals, and often have serious issues scoring the ball when they can't get out in transition off their steals. Still, if last year is any indication, McNeese is not a team that a 5-seed wants to see next to their name on Selection Sunday.
